trajectory_summary: The call never built energy. Meg opened into a legitimacy gap she couldn't close, encountered a soft move-soon objection, and self-terminated before attempting any reframe. The one moment of genuine customer engagement — Robert's Laurie/neighbor question at 01:44 — arrived after Meg had already decided to exit and was handled with a non-answer. The call ended with no appointment, no callback, and no forward motion of any kind.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as 'unknown.' Based on the transcript, the correct tag is 'disqualified — self-terminated by rep.' However, the disqualification is contestable: Robert's move timeline is 'maybe this summer if I get the right price,' which is conditional and months away. A rep with a future-state hook or a curiosity-creation move could have kept this lead warm. The self-termination was Meg's choice, not a hard DQ condition.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Conditional-objection exit discipline — owning the next contact
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. No. It totally gets you there. Well, what I could do, I'll go ahead and, I'll take you off our list. If you end up staying and you wanna look at solar options, just give me a call and let me know.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Meg self-terminates on a conditional objection ('maybe this summer if I get the right price') without attempting a reframe, a curiosity hook, or a future-touch she controls. The schema's objection_inversion framework and soft_outcome_distinction directive both apply here. 'Give me a call' places all forward motion on the customer and effectively closes the lead. The coaching goal is to build the habit of owning the next contact even on soft exits — a 30-second future-state hook costs nothing and keeps the lead alive.

key_moments.quote: You filled out a solar form online looking at solar panels for your home. Hello, Sean? No. I did.
key_moments.stewart_read: Meg addresses the customer as 'Sean' mid-sentence — a name that does not match 'Robert,' the name she used at 00:03. Speaker_2 is present on the call, so the cause is uncertain: this may be a CRM data mismatch, a misread of the script, or a momentary address to the third party. What is clear is the effect: Robert hears a wrong name layered on top of a form he doesn't recognize, compounding the legitimacy gap before the pitch has started. The schema's protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number directive calls for honest specificity precisely because vagueness increases suspicion.

key_moments.quote: I gotcha. Or go to Florida.
key_moments.stewart_read: Speaker_2 interjects here with a comment that tracks the conversation ('I gotcha. Or go to Florida.') — this is an engaged third party, not background noise. Per schema protocols.spouse_decision and protocols.adult_child_decision_maker, any co-present party who engages in the conversation is a potential co-decision-maker signal. Meg never acknowledges speaker_2, never asks who they are, and never checks whether they are on the title or involved in the decision. The call proceeds as if only Robert is present.

key_moments.quote: Oh, no. Maybe this summer if I get the right price on my end.
key_moments.stewart_read: Robert's move is conditional — 'if I get the right price.' This is not a hard disqualifier; it is a soft, future-state objection. The schema's objection_inversion framework is directly applicable here: the flip available is something like 'Some sellers find it worth knowing what a design looks like before they list — want to see what it would show?' This creates curiosity without making a factual claim about resale outcomes. Meg instead treats the conditional as a hard DQ and self-terminates. Strategic read: there is no evidence Meg was setting up a later move — she exits immediately and offers no callback or future hook.

key_moments.quote: You did any houses near me in the area?
key_moments.stewart_read: Robert asks unprompted whether Ion has done work near him — this is a social-proof inquiry, not a stall. It signals residual curiosity about solar even after the move objection. He then volunteers his neighbor Laurie's name at 01:44. This is the highest-engagement moment in the call. Meg's response ('Am I able to look them up in my system? Alright. Understood.') is a non-answer that extinguishes the signal entirely. A rep who recognized this as an enthusiasm signal would have used it as a re-entry point: 'I can't pull up individual accounts, but if Laurie's your neighbor, she's probably seen the same kind of savings you would — that's actually why I wanted to talk to you.'
